story:
Honking geese carve a giant V across a late September dawn as Tralley unloads a pickup beside a transmission tower on a hill. The sky reddens; the air is clean and thin. Rucker sits in the cab, eyes on his phone, then looks up just long enough to track the formation’s precision before dipping back to the glow in his hand. The screen’s the thing.

Tralley bangs the truck bed with his toolbox. Rucker lifts a finger—his be-with-you-in-a-sec signal—and finally joins him. “Beautiful morning,” he says, full lungs and easy grin. Tralley’s sarcasm—“Get that from Google?”—slides off. “Naw,” Rucker says. “Direct from the photons on yonder horizon. You gotta get the soup wherever it’s dished.”

They shoulder equipment toward the squat, bunker-like building by the tower. New relays today, meant to dampen the hiss and hum. “They’ll help,” Tralley says. “But there’s always noise. The goal is to push it to the background. Folks don’t want to hear their own blood.”

Rucker nods, pleased. “Noise is information too. Everything is information. Sometimes listening to the noise reminds us reality’s a strange mix—uncertainty, randomness, probability. Position, time, energy. Source, medium, destination. It’s all about the bit. We are the signal.” He says it lightly, almost musically, like he’s saying it to the morning itself.

At the door, Rucker taps his phone to the pad. Locks clack and unlatch. He pauses, holding the door. “Might as well tell you. I’ve been approved for encoding.”

Tralley’s eyes widen, then narrow. “You’re not going to do that.” It lands like an order.

“’Course I am.”

“A walking relay station? A human piece of infrastructure?” Tralley’s voice almost breaks. “You’ll be always connected. Always locatable.”

Rucker doesn’t flinch. “We hire people to carry external hardware. This is just embedded. A network no one can take down. Towers like this? Targets. Relics. We’re already the information. Now we become the channel.”

“That’s a cog in the machine. A chip on the board,” Tralley says. “Why let the company inside your person?”

“Integral beats isolated,” Rucker answers. “Mutual dependence isn’t bad. Openness and transparency are the way forward. Lone wolves worry me. Isolation and secrets make miscreants.” He says it calmly, as if reciting a field rule: look back sometimes—safety, you know.

Tralley stares east. Peaks wear early snow. The sun edges over. He can’t square Rucker’s easygoing, grounded mind with this choice. “You’re so damn smart. Why compromise?”

“Compromise?” Rucker smiles. “This is examined. This is transcendence. You saw the geese. Someone takes the point. Maybe I’ll rue it. Maybe not. I’m thinking about a different tomorrow. Information is all about position, and I’m putting myself front and center. In a decade, this tower will be obsolete, but I might be sitting like a lotus on a mountaintop—any mountaintop—and what makes humanity hum will pass through me. I dig that.”

“Could be you’re digging your own grave,” Tralley says.

“Could be.” Rucker shrugs. “It’s all heat death to me.”

They stand with a load of electronics between them and a gulf of uncertainty, the flock already past and the day fully beginning. “Let’s get our job done,” Tralley says at last.

“That’s what we do,” Rucker answers, and holds the dull metal door for him. “After you.”
